headline: African American Communities Celebrate Lincoln's Birthday
body:
The year of our Lord 1909 dawned upon Helena, Montana, a city where the pulse of progress beat unevenly, especially for its African American citizens. Yet, on the twelfth day of February, a different rhythm stirred the air, a cadence of remembrance and resolve. It was the centennial of Abraham Lincoln’s birth, and for the Black communities across Montana, particularly in Helena, this was no mere date on a calendar; it was a rallying cry, a testament to an enduring hope. The Montana Plaindealer, that vigilant sentinel of truth under the editorship of Joseph B. Bass, chronicled these gatherings, understanding their profound significance. These were not simply festive assemblies; they were vital organs of a community striving for recognition, for justice, for the very breath of equality in a state that often seemed to hold it hostage.

In Helena, where over four hundred African Americans comprised a notable three percent of the city’s populace, the observances were particularly vibrant. The St. James AME Zion Church and the Second Baptist Church, pillars of the community, became centers of fervent activity. Here, speeches were delivered, their words sharp and unyielding, dissecting the injustices of the day even as they lauded the legacy of the Great Emancipator. Musical performances, soulful and stirring, wove through the halls, binding spirits in a shared narrative of struggle and aspiration. Community meals, simple yet profound, fostered a solidarity that transcended the daily indignities faced by Black Montanans. These were moments when identity was affirmed, when the collective voice, often stifled, found its resonant chord.

Bass, through the Plaindealer, was an unyielding advocate. He had, in the previous year, railed against the blatant prejudice that allowed establishments to hang signs proclaiming ‘No Colored Trade Solicited.’ He had exposed the hypocrisy of those who would deny a Black man a meal but eagerly seek his company at a poker table, to filch his coin. He had celebrated the Supreme Court’s decision striking down a law that sought to prohibit colored Elks from wearing their insignia, declaring with righteous indignation, “NO JIM CROW LAWS FOR MONTANA.” This was the backdrop against which Lincoln’s birthday was celebrated: a constant, grinding struggle against systemic and casual racism, a fight for the basic dignities of citizenship.

The celebrations of February 12, 1909, were thus imbued with a dual purpose. They were, on one hand, an expression of profound gratitude for the man whose proclamation had shattered the chains of chattel slavery. On the other, they were a powerful, annual reaffirmation of the ongoing fight for true freedom, for the full realization of Lincoln’s promise in the daily lives of Black Montanans. They were a platform for discussing civil rights, for strategizing social progress, and for demanding a more just future. As the Plaindealer itself noted on February 12, 1909, page 1, these celebrations were a vital part of the cultural and historical fabric, demonstrating a resilience that refused to be broken, and an enduring commitment to the ideals of liberty and justice, even when those ideals seemed distant and hard-won. The spirit of these gatherings, the unwavering belief in a better tomorrow, echoed the very words of Bass himself, who, in his inaugural editorial, pledged that his enterprise would “at all times strive for a greater Helena, and for mutual progress of all the people of the community, and for a greater activity of our people in the business world.” It was a world they were determined to build, brick by painful brick, in the face of indifference and outright hostility.
